CURRENT AFFAIRS,

Carson day was an utter failure in Dublin. We only
saw two Jacks flying in honour of the occasion. One
was flying over some Non-Catholic chapel in Dundrum
and the other was rammed out of a small cottage in
the Stillorgan diection—probably owned by some
labourer who got & job from some bigot because he was
a “‘ true blue.”

The pantomime in Belfast is over, and the
Nationaligts must be pretity well as much bored by the

whole thing in the newspapets as we are. ~ So far as
Great Britain is concerned Carson’s theaftricals appear
to have fallen very flat. The fellow who got the
job to write the ““ Irish Times’ " leading article on the
affair certainly had & difficult job, and he did it very
badly and ridiculously. He said nothing about Dublin
at ﬂﬁ; ‘we wonder had even the “ Irish Times ”’ o Jack

out? It will be remembered that on the occasion of
Asquith’s visit the balcony over its shop front in West-
moreland Street was thronged with enthusiastic Home
Rulers. We suppose the editor, the *“ Saved >’ Healy,
did not sign the wee covenant as he hails from
Drogheda.  The editor of the “‘ Irish Times '’ would
look dangerous slinking along'in bluchers and a cocked
hat, with a terrible wooden rifle in one hand and a pen
in the other, so perhaps it is well for the poor Papists
that he was not eligible, owing to perverse geography,
to sign on.  If he dressed up as Cromwell he would
frighten the crows, not to say us poor Papists.

We wonder was it the *“ Saved '’ Healy himself who
wrote the leading article; it is puerile and silly enough
anyway. It says: ‘““ The day was observed in the
strictest sense as a holy-day.”” Is there not a dan-
gerous Papist smack about that term  ‘‘ holy-day ”?
And what are we to style Carson in view of Saturday last
being a Belfast * holy-day’’? General Carson had given
way to King Carson, but now it would seem that nothing
short of Saint Carson will meet the case. The ‘‘ Irish
Times’’’ leading article tells us that the Carsonites
‘“ have set their hands to the plough, and they will not.
turn back.”” The plough, eh? We thought it was the
wooden rifle and the iron rivet. It certainly would be
better for themselves and for odd defenceless Papists
cornered by a mob of them if they stuck to the plough.
Writing of the *‘ civic harmony ** of Belfast, whatever
that is, it is hard to know whether the following is
prose or poetry: ‘‘ It was as if the spirit of the city
had leaped, on this historic occasion, into material
form.”” The opal hushers may take a back seat after
that.  Our friend the Hairy Fairy of Plunkett House
ought to paint that at once. Further down in the
article we read :—*“ At the great services bishops of the
Church of Treland and leaders of the Presbyterian and
Methodist Churches mingled their prayers for Ireland.’”
We have italicised ‘ for Ireland.””

The charges for driving in Dublin are once more
brought prominently before the public by the fines and
remarks of a Dublin police magistrate. May we take
the opportunity of again making our suggestion that
the cabs and outsides should adopt the taxi system
and have a minimum, and so much per mile after-
wards.  We understand that one person can drive
from one point in the city to the farthest point within
the boundary for the ]egaf fare of sixpence. That may
be a legal fare, but it is not a rational one. In so far
-abby has a grievance. But we think most people
are shy of taking a car in view of the uncertainty as
to what amount will refrain cabby from kicking up a
shindy at the end of the journey. That is certainly
our position.  We never take a car or cab except when
we absolutely cannot help it, and when we do it is not
the legal fare, of which, like most people, we are
ignorant, that we tender, but such fare as we in our
judgment consider will not involve us in an unseemly
dispute at the end. The tramway fares in Dublin—
particularly the absence of half fares for children—are
extortionate enough.  But yet if one wishes to go to
Dalkey from the Pillar, he knows that it is 5d. and
not a penny more. If he were in doubt whether 5d.,
1s., or 1s. 6d. might be accepted with grumbling and
possibly abuse he, in many cases, would simply not
make the journey. If legal fares are not enough let
them be revised; if they are enough they should be
adhered to. Our view is that there should be a mini-

~ mum charge for a car, nnd‘ beyond that the charge

should be regulated by the extent of the further drive,
and that exfent. should be registered as in the case
of taxi cabs. Many of- the cabs, and paxticularly the
horses between their shafts, are not ornaments to the
city. The Anti-Taxi Cab League,:so far as.we know,
is>a purely negptive association, and its horsemen aft
Nationalist processions are at once interested and un-

thing constructive and practical to meet the mnew
situation in which their trade finds itself placed.

When Home Rule is established the Unionists in Ire-
land are not to ‘‘recognise” it. There may be
Orangemen less amenable to the bailiff than our
“ extreme "'—Mr, John Sweetman, who doesn’t
““recognise  the Union, but who pays his income
tax,. as if he refused his furniture would be seized—
and possibly there may not be any. Be that as it
may, the Post Office—with or without the jobbed
Norway man—will be under the Trish Parliament, and,
as the Unionists will not *‘ recognise '’ that Parliament,
they cannot use its postage stamps. The Orangemen
must then carry their own letters, and if a Unionist mer-
chant in the country wants to send an order to Belfast
he must send it by train or motor or, perhaps, by one
of Carson’s mounted foot. If an unscrupulous man
owes a Unionist, say, £100, he can refuse to pay, and
put out his tongue his debtor, for the Unionist, not
*‘ recognising "’ the Trish Parliament, cannot take legal
proceedings to recover the debt. It will be a great
time for Unionist swindlers, for, as Belfast is famous,
or infamous, for fraudulent bankrupteies: and other
corruptions, we may take it that there will be many
Belfast swindlers ready to take advantage of the state
of things, wherein Belfast Unionists will not ‘‘ recog-
nise *’ the Irish Courts. If a Belfast blackguard loots
a shop in Dublin, the shopkeeper, if he happens to be
a ‘‘ Covenanter '’ cannot call a policeman, for the
Dublin Police will be subject to the Irish Parliament,
and, as such cannot be ‘‘ recognised.”  One could go
on for columns relating the curious things that must
happen when the ‘‘ Covenanters’’ refuse to ‘‘recog-
nise '’ the Irish Parliament. Our Mr. Sweetman, who
‘pays his income tax, as if he didn’t his furniture would
be seized, must feel special enjoyment at the prospects
that awaits the ‘‘ Covenanters.”

September 26th, given by BEgcrton, and reporbed in
the subservient ‘‘ Treeman,” was Mr. A. H. Norway.

. Mr. James McMahon, a postal official, made a speech

praising up Tgerton, .and the latter gentleman—who
failed in his attempt to malke us pay 14d. instead of a

ha’penny inland postage on our second last Christmas -

Number—made remarks of the usual type.

Tgerton said: * He felt sure the position he was
now vacating would be well and ably filled by his
successor, Mr. A, H. Norway.” Not a word of
protest at the passing over of his own men.

The Urlingford Guardians drew a letter from John
Dillon, which is set out in full in the columns of the
subservient ““ I'reeman.’”’  John Redmond’s secretary,
we are told in the ‘“ T'reeman >’ report, ““ acknowledges
receipt of resolution on the same subject;” but the
terms of the letter are not set out. The -portion of
Dillon’s letter referring to the atrocious Norway. job
is as follows:— 3

As regards the resolution concgrning the
appointment to the Secretaryship of the Post
Office, we cannot accept responsibility under
the present system  for Government appoint-
ments; but I feel bound to say that I cannot
agree with the principle laid down in the resolu-
tion that the appointment of any Irishman to the
office would necessarily be a better appointment
than any BEnglishman; nor do I think it would be
wise for us, even if we could, to set up the prin-
ciple that the higher posts in the Civil vice in
this country should absolutely ed to
Englishmen. A similar principle enforced in
Great Britain would inflict terrible injury on our
own countrymen, who have been singularly
successful in the Civil Service of Great Britain.

This is too thin, Mr., Dillon. No one asked M.
Dillon to accept responsibility for the Norway job:
what we and others naturally and reasonably
expected him to do was to publicly protest against it..
We have not seen the resolution of the Urlingford
statesmen, but the pro against the Norway job
does not involve the assertion of the principle that
** the appointment of any Trishman to the office would
necessarily be a better appointment than any English-
man.”’ TIf the Urlingford statesmen put that in their
resolution they may, for all we know to the contrary,
have done so in order to allow Dillon to make a cheap
point. This is a concrete case of planting a man over
here who knows nothing of Ireland, and supplanting
Trish officials who have been working the business for
years. The reply of Mr. Dillon is utterly unconvine-
ing, but it shows that he has been subservient to the
plot of Mr. Samuel, the Jew, to dump an importation,
said to be a Freemason, over the heads of the officials

in the Irish Post Office, one of whom, in the natural-

way of promotion, should have been.promoted to the
Secretaryship.”  Dillon and the Party don’t accept
responsibility under the present system for Govern-
ment appointments.  But supposing the * Liberal *
Government had proposed to make Tim Healy
Solicitor-General, would they take it as meekly as
they take the Norway job? Did they exercise no voice,
if not veto, in the appointments of the Solicitor-
General, the Attorney-General, and in some of the
TInsurance posts ?

The thing that Dillon’s letter shows to us is that
he was practically party to the job: and that fact
makes us fearful that in the Home Rule deal, if it

We will not delay now to point out at any length the
un-Irish’ point of view in M. Dillon’s concern for those
persons who leave Ireland for service abroad. An
Irishman who settles in Eugland adopts England as his
-country and there is the end of him—he is lost to
Ireland. If Irishmen can equalise matters by getting
good jobs in England, then Hu whole case for Treland
an entity is thrown over. It is ouly on the Treland an
entity point of view, so far as we can see, that Ireland
can make good its claim concerning the over-taxation
question. If an Tris n may be successful, from
the Trish point of view, in England, then we must adopt
the Great Britain and Ireland entity and the over-taxa-
tion question is wiped out, and indeed the case for Home
Rule and Ireland a Nation goes as a natural and logical
consequence 2lso. .

We are under the impression Umk wmc (ime ago we
drew attention to the very much ‘‘ Saved’’ Cork Sav-
ings Bank. There was recently a mmnc) for a clerk
in that institution.  Of course, a Catholic did not
get it. Here is how a correspondent puts it:—
“TI beg to bring to your notice the latest piece of
jobbery—the reward for our tolerance proving, which has
taken place in the city of Cork. T refer to the recent
appointment of a junior clerk to the Cork Savings Bank.
It is remarkable that in this benighted ‘ South and
West ’* that this Bank—a typical example of our public
companies—should show that the number of persecuted
‘ Saved ’ to-be 66 on a board of 106, and what is still
more remarkable is the fact that they could outnumber
their persecutors by more than 2 to 1 when it comes to
filling a post. There were three applicants—two ‘Saved”
and one Catholic, and the two ‘Saved’ could manage
-to be in the field eight or nine days before the Catholic
knew anything of the position, and then the examina-
tion—bless the word—is announced four days before-
hand. Tt is somewhat enlightening to go into the

details of the farcical proccdulo W 'll(h the management

There are seven or eight positions in the Bank ranging

O’Brien mouthing conciliation and olive branches, and
the only result of his preaching that we can sce is the
open persecution of our Catholic brethren in the North,
where they are in a minority, and the secret ousting of
them from every position worth having in the South.
We have been too long swallowing platitudes of the
O’Brienite type, and it is about time that we now began
to take for ourselves some of those things which are ours
by right.”

the impertinent attitude of stand and deliver to the

vinced that these bounders badly need being put in

humane medical syndicate magnanimously announce
that this boyeott will not operate in cases of *“ extreme
urgency.””  Most noble Sawbones!  So if a man blead-
ing to death and belonging to, say, the A.0.H., if the

be stopped. Take care these bounding boycotters
don’t get their gums lanced by the public. Even
though Ireland is largely inhabited by a green people,
they may not prove such an easy prey to the social
upstarts ‘and professional more or less incompetents
who largely comprise the rank and file of Sawbones in
Ireland.

A certain gentleman was speaking to us the other
day, and he has very little respect for many of the
“doctors *’ in Treland, and is enraged at their attempted
boycotting tricks We gathered that his view is, and
many will be inclined to agree with it, that if a lot of
““doctors " went on sfrike there would be a large drop
in the death-rate. He told us a story of an incident
that occurred years ago in which his father and a pro-
vineial ““ doctor >’ were concerned. A member of the
family became dangerously ill during the night and his
father went for the ‘‘ doctor.”” The doctor was in bed
and replied that he would call round in the morning.
It was an urgent case, and our friend’s father was not
soing to allow one of his family to die, perhaps, because
Sawbones did not wish to come out of bed. He went
out on the road and collected stones and broke the
windows in Sawbones house. This frightened the old
chap, and to make a long story short—and to set it
down as we heard it—the doctor trotted off to the case
in his night shirvt, and our friend’s father told him that
if he hadn’t come he would have broken every bone in
his body as well as every window in his house.

is going.  The g;reat social ideal should be to keep the

. minimum standard of comfort .as high as possible.

There is a tendency to beat it down, and that tendency

" needs to be countered in every reasonable way. The

tendency works in many ways. If the Insurance Act
put the Triendly Societies in a position to ‘pay more
than they paid previously to Sawbones, there would be
a natural tendency for Sawbones to cut into the margin
and for the members of the Friendly Socicties to give
away any economic advantage that the Act may give
them. This has to be guarded against. In the old
tenant-at-will days if a farmer was industrious the
landlord put up the rent; in these days if the working
classes get an advantage, Sawbones may want to take
it and form a ‘ stand and deliver ”’ boycotting ring to
gain his ends.  We have only within the past few
days had our interest aroused in the doctors’ tricks,
but we will keep an eye on them now and follow as
closely as we conveniently can their *stand and
deliver *’ money-grubbing tactics towards the Friendly
Societies.

Now that Belfast travellers are alleged to be meet-
ing with some very natural resentments in the ‘“ South
and West,”” it may happen that Dublin wholesale
houses are getting extru trade.  But some Dublin
wholesale houses would want a talking to also. Some
time back we referred to the colour of the staff of
Crowe, Wilson and Co., of Bridge Street, the wholesale
drapers. If these people are to get extra custom
because Belfast is losing it, it would be well for traders
to keep an eye out that any future vacancies that may
occur in the ranks of its travellers and buyers and staff
generally are not closed against eligible applicants who
may happen to be Catholics.

St. Jude’s Church Defenders Temperance Loyal
Orange Lodge 818 had their annual re-union recently.
It is said to be one of the most flourishing lodges in
the Ballynafeigh district. It was held in the local
Orange Hall, and there was a large attendance of the
‘ brethren.””  Amongst those present was Brother
R. H. Noble, Secretary of the Orange and Protestant
Triendly Society. A non-Catholic clergyman, Canon
Davis, we read, in the course of a short speech, said
thére was not one nation in Ireland, but two, and they
were especially interested in that nation they repre-
sented, the Protestant community of the land, who
were faced with grave danger. They knew that the
nefarious scheme of Home Rule with which they were
threatened, would destroy their prosperity.  Belfast
had developed in marvellous fashion as a result of the
industry and thrift of the majority of the population,
and when its well-being was in danger it was no wonder

I am afraid, too, that a self-satisfied contempt for the
young, Anglified generation—for his own children, that
Is to say—smiles through much of his participation in
Revival functions.  What ave we to do with him ?

The trouble is that the Irish speaker won’t speak
Trish to his childven. He takes the children to a pen
amicably enough and occasionally teaches them a song
or a story in preparation for the e, but he would
tale them to a circus in much the same spivit, and, as
for talking Trish to them on the way home as the result
of the Peip or the speeches made thereat, you might as
well expect to see him tumbling somersaults with them
along the road on the way home from the circus. And
the worst of the trouble is that it is not out of conviction
or with a positive desire to kill Irish he acts as he acts.
If he held any positive conviction in the matter you
might at least argue with him. As it is, you may not
argue with him. For it takes two to argue. He won’t
be one of the two. Like an English minister on *‘ Irish”’
nights in the House of Commons, he listens to the Irish
case, perhaps malkes a few casual remarks in reply,
then turns away to go about his business and thinks
no more about the matter. Again, what are we to do
with him ?

Our editor says—pepper him.  Cordially I agree,
but we would do well to take his range and our aim
with discrimination before we open fire. For there are
more types than one of Irish speakers, and there are
different shades of guilt attaching to them. Suppose
we sort the types roughly out.

First, there is the Ballyvourney and Ballyferriter,
Ring and Rosmuc type. Now, as far as my experience
goes, that type doesn’t call for much pepper. It is all
right or nearly so. The people in the districts men-
tioned speak a certain amount of English to their chil-
dren, but they do so, I think, only for export. They

‘speak Irish to them also, and the children know Irish.

I admit that English is increasing and Irish diminish-
ing in every household in the districts with every com-
ing year, but that needn’t either surprise or dishearten
us. For there is such a thing as what I may call a

" momentum of decay that hurries nationalities and civi-

lisations down hill once they begin to lose ground, and
you've got to brake that momentum to a stand still
‘before you can start regaining the lost ground. In any
district, however, in which Irish is being spoken to the
children it is foolish to talk of much ground having been
lost. No, in the real Irish districts Manchester civili-
sation has not as yet got any grip thut need male us
wince, and if only the schools were Trish the children,
and with them the districts, would, T think, be saved
to Treland permanently.

speaker. Who, then, is to act the candid friend to the
man who can speak Irish to his children and won’t?
There are plenty of us to do it. In the provincial Press,
week after week, there ave reports of Feiseanna,
articles about the ‘* Language of the Gael,”” and so on,
and, as a rule, they are wishy-washy, colourless things,
enough in fact to disgust a masculine Gael.  Suppose
's of those reports and articles stood out be-
fore their subject in future and, instead of slobbering
the ““ old tongue ’’ with treacle, told the spealers who
can and won’t speak that tongue the truth! Tvery
line, let us remember, of those papers is read during
the long Sundays by everyone of every household in
the country, and, to give the Gael his due, he has a
literary taste that will like the smack of an article, or
a speech that examines his conscience for him with
insight. Speaking of consciences, too, there is no
need for the language missioner to lose his own or to
ruffle the backsliding Irish speaker’s temper. We are
all sinners more or less, but some of us must do the
preaching. So that, be up all you Irish-Treland writers
and, in Irish and in English, tell the Irish speaker the
unpleasant truth rather than the pleasant lie during
the coming winter. ~Writers of the Lraper, might I
suggest to you too to give Irish-Treland more of your
attention in future than you bave given it during some
months past.

One word in conclusion. Writers or speakers criti-
cal of the Irish speaker must be prepared to do without
an answer to their charges. The Irish speakers un-
fortunately differ from the National teachers in that
respect.

IMPRESSIONS OF LISDOONVARNA. U

- ISDOONVARNA is a pleasant oasis, seated on a
vast plateau of moorland. In rainy weather the
place looks dismal enough; bub on bright days, when
the sunlight gleams on the whitewashed walls of its
hotels and boarding-houses, the famous little town -
looks gay, attractive, and even stately. ~Away to the
north, the heathery ridge of Mount Elva shuts out the
sky, while in the far south, the flat summit oﬁ Mount
Callan rises above the plain. To the west, just per-
ceptible from the highest of Lisdoonvarna’s hotels, lies
the broad expanse of the Atlantic. On the east are the
barren crags and smiling, fertile valleys of the Burren
range, the delight of sundry votaries of botany and
geology. 3

Trom the gentle eminence on.which it stands, this
far-famed health resort looks over a wide stretch of
damp and treeless, but not unpopulous country. All
this region, and much farther than the eye can reach,
was for many ages the domain of the O’Loghlens,
Princes of Burren. IEspecially in its eastern-part, the
country is still studded with the ruins of their castles,
seventeen of which have been counted by inquiring
antiquarians.  Some members of the clan have risen
to eminence in law and politics, both in Ireland and
Australia. The names of Sir Colman and Sir Michael
O’Loghlen are still remembered in the homeland ; and,
on the far side of the round world, the Hon. Larry and
the Hon. James Vincent O’Loghlen have played pro-
minent, though dissimilar, parts in the politics of South
Australia. It is a pity that no industrious son of the
sept has taken pains to chronicle the deeds and vicissi-
tudes of the O’Loghlens; their chequered history is an
interesting and honourable one.

Perhaps, the first thing that strikes a stranger in
Lisdoonvarna is the bare, treeless character of the sur-
rounding country. In this respect the place contrasts
very unfavourably with many portions of Leinster. The
whole aspect of the landscape around Lisdoonvarna is
marred by the absence of woodlands. One sees only
a bleak, wind-swept, shelterless plain; the entire
country—though it contains some excellent fattening
lands—looks heathery, marshy, boggy, and uninviting.
A liberal sprinkling of plantations here and there would
effect an immense improvement in the appearance of
the district. It would be good business for the Lis-
doonvarna folk to persuade their agricultural neigh-
bours to plant as many trees as possible. ~Such pian-
tations would afford sorely-needed shelter for the
farmers’ sheep and cattle; the trees themselves would
be worth a good deal of money in the course of years;
and the outlook from Lisdoonvarna would be vastly
pleasanter and more cheerful. It is said, doubtless
with some truth, that many species of saplings would
be killed by the keen Atlantic breezes.  But it is
surely possible to discover species suited to the soil and
climate—Scotch firs, perhaps, might be tried with ad-
vantage.  Anyhow, it is abundantly clear that Lis-
doonvarna needs an Arbor Day, and needs it badly.

Most of the visitors to Lisdoonvarna are Catholics,
and the local church is well patronised. It is very edify-
ing to observe devout crowds of visitors attending Mass
on Sundays, and even on week-day mornings, and pre-
paring for Confession on Saturday evenings. A num-
ber of highly - praiseworthy improvements have been
carried out in the church within the last dozen years.
The parish priest, Father Conroy, is a fluent Gaelic
speaker; he is said to be one of the few priests in the
diocese who can preach a thoroughly good Ivish sermon.

The Improvement Committee deserves a full meed
of praise for its efforts to promote the progress of the
town. The surroundings of the sulphur, magnesia, and
other springs show a distinet and most welcome im-
provement. Much has been done, and much more
remains' yet to be done. For one thing, the Commit-
tee might lawfully pay its officials a minimum wage of
two shillings per day. Besides, somebody should have
‘the enterprise to start a motor omnibus service between
Lisdoonvarna and Ennistymon; it would be a mighty
boon to many who are tived of ** long cars,”” and of that
most uncomfortable and dangerous of vehicles, the
Tvish ““ side-car.””

The most urgent, .perhaps, of Lisdoonvarna’s needs
is a public hall. It seems incredible that a health
resort which has been assiduously advertised, and
which has attracted multitudes of tors and health-
seekers during forty years and more, should still be
minus a hall for public entertainment. A hall in which
lectures, concerts, balls, and picture shows could be
given is a very great desideratum ; imperatively
necessary in such a place as Lisdoonvarna. If such a
ball were in existence, the Committec might make
arrangements with various notabiliti o deliver lec-
tures there, from time to time, du the holiday
season; and ardent Gaelic Leaguers might utilise the
place as a platform from which to expound the
‘to auditors from all parts of Ireland. = As mafte
at present, the complaint is unive: that Lis-
doonvarna is absolutely devoid of entertainments, ex-
cept such impromptu entertainments as the hotels
provide.

In fairmess to members of the Improvement Com-
mittee, it must be acknowledged that they appear to
be quite alive to the need for providing some such
building.  The story goes that they approached a cer-
tain landowner, who refused to sell them a site, except
at the exorbitant price of £100 per acre. The indivi
dual in question could well afford to offer a site gratis;
and a man of public spirit would doubtless have done
s0. This lack of public spirit, coupled with insatiable
-greed and meanness, has much to do with the stagnant
condition of Irish towns. If the holders of unimproved
lands in the immediate vicinity of towns were smartly
‘taxed, it would put an end fo a good deal of the stagna-
tion that at present exists.

This year, at all events, a cheering symptom of pro-
gress has been witnessed in Lisdoonvarna. A branch
of the Hibernian Society has been formed in the town.
Tt is difficult to understand why some few priests in
Treland adopt a hostile attitude towards the Hibernians.
‘Such an atfitude is certainly the result of prejudice, or
Under proper
-guidance from the clergy, the Hibernian Society affords
-an admirable means of getting the Catholic young men
of the district (1) to frequent the Sacraments; (2) to
keep clear of the publichouse; (3) to counteract the
‘baneful influence of Freemasonry; (4) to help Catholics
in business; (5) to create a demand for goods of Irish
manufacture; and (6) to encourage the spread of
Catholic literature among the people. These are
matters of vital importance; and it will be extremely
hard to point out any organised body, ofher than the
Hibernians, which can do all these things effectively.

THE CENSUS SUMMARIES.— ULSTER.

rl‘ HE Census Commissioners have just issued their

Provincial Summaries, which are suggestive and
full of interest. The population of Ulster, it appears,
is slightly higher than the figure given in the prelimi-
nary Report, the persons enumerated being 1,581,696
instead of 1,578,572. As compared with 1901 the
population has practically remained stationary, the
falling away being only 1,230, or 0.07 per cent., the
increase of nearly 386,000 in the population of Belfast
having only just failed to bring about a provincial in-
crease, and neutralise the adverse effect of an increased
decennial emigration.

The number of emigrants during the ten years ended
31st March, 1911, who stated that they were from the
Province of Ulster amounted to 106,587 (58,298 males
and 48,289 females); the number for the previous
decade being only 86,455, or over 20,000 less. The
emigration for the sixty years ending 31st March last
makes the appalling total of 1,192,177—or to more than
three times the present population of the City of Bel-
fast, or three-fourths of that of the province. Since
1841 (when the population was 2,389,263) there has
been a decline of over 51 per cent.

Notwithstanding the increased emigration during the
last ten years the Catholics (except in Belfast and
neighbourhood, where they have slightly receded) ave in

- FOLLOWING the precedent set at King Edward’s

employers generously gave a holiday to their workers,
and as ungenerously withheld the great ‘‘Covenanting’
< day’s pay.  Accordingly, glazed paper bunting did

duty for the real thing, and broom handles were good:

substitutes for ** Wands of Office ” in the hands of
the weedy defenders of the City Hall. Inside this
public building, erected by the rate-paying citizens,
Catholic as well as non-Catholic, the bands incessantly
played the now first favourite rag-time tune, ‘‘ Dolly’s
Brae,”” which has for its gentle refrain, *“ We'll kick
the Pope **; and it is well to remember in this connec-
tion that Mr. Brennan, the city organist, kindly
‘‘obliged "’ with this highly Christian ditty on the
Mulholland organ in Uls Hall.  So when the
** tolerant ' promoters of various Feiseanna hear of
this performance, they may hesitate at again engaging
this musical gentleman.

Saturnine King Edward Carson—surrounded by his
burlesque opera outriders, and flashing heliograph
messages to ‘‘ Crozier, Armagh’’—was surely struck
with the tame, flat gathering, which required all the
wine-flushed toadies’ frantic rousing to work up to
proper concert pitch,  Belfast sent its bigot-mouthing
crowds to the city docks to speed the parting guest,
and the improving sight of the crowded boat dining-
room, with the raucous-voiced lawyer toasting a
bumper, provoked a new form of objurgation which
drowned bands and cheers, to wit, a hoarse, thunderous
chant of the legend which the ill-fated ‘* Titanic *’ bore
on its departure from Belfast, ‘“To Hell with the
Pope.”” - So to measured time this Belfast password
is set, and only crystallises the common speech of the
public works throughout the bigot city.

hooliganesses, who roared the curse into the ears of the
unfortunate Catholic workers, and not content with

with vengeful reference to Home Rule, and inquiry

CURRENT AFFAIRS.

On Tuesday a gentleman brought us a letter ad-
dressed altogether in Trish. It was posted in London
-on September 19th and was delivered in Rathgar on
the evening of September 21st—21 days. It was sub-
jected to two translations. Some official in London
was evidently able to make out that it was intended
for Dublin, Ireland. So he blue-pencilled those words
upon it and sent it off. Tn Dublin the rest of the
address was translated and the letter duly reached its
destination. The inadequate manner in which the
Post Office in Treland deals with letters in Trish is an
old standing theme, and now—John Dillon and the
Party not protesting—an Englishman has been dumped
over here to be Secretary.

In ““ Kemp's Gazette,” of September 30th, we find
two pages under the heading * Lists of Principal Cre-
ditors, Reports of Meetings of Creditors, Circulars, and
